figures.caption: Fig. 5. An overall pipeline of the inference process of SynHAT. In the first stage, we input Gaussian noise into the trained Coarse-HADiff in the inference process, and output synthetic coarse-grained latent ST traces. To address the issues of missing activities and misaligned timestamps in coarse-grained traces, the following stage 2 comprises three modules, namely the Behavior Pattern Extraction Module (BPEM), the Fine-HADiff, and Semantic Alignment, to produce synthetic fine-grained HATs.
Table 2. Fidelity Evaluation. The best results on each dataset are in bold, and the second-best results are underlined.
Fig. 13. POI-anchored activity distributions of different methods in four cities. The brighter the color is, the more frequently the POI is visited.
Fig. 2. (a, b) Visualizations of interval and length distributions of real HAT data and interpolated ones at different granularities. (c) Computational costs at different granularities.
Fig. 12. Activity transition density matrices of real and synthetic HAT datasets in NYC and TKY.
Fig. 14. Privacy-preserving statistics for HAT generation in NYC and TKY. P95 denotes the 95th percentile of similarity values in the synthetic datasets.
Table 5. Ablation studies on NYC and ATX. Reported values are Jensen–Shannon divergence (JSD); lower is better.

Fig. 9. Privacy-preserving performance of HAT generation across four cities. The left vertical yellow line denotes the ideal privacy target (best-case non-memorization), and the right vertical orange line denotes the similarity CDF of the original training HATs. 𝑡𝑟𝑠 and 𝑡𝑟𝑡 denote the spatial and temporal thresholds for similarity computation. The closer a model’s similarity CDF is to the yellow line and the farther from the orange line, the stronger its privacy-preserving effectiveness.
Fig. 6. Denoising Network in Coarse-grained HADiff and Fine-grained HADiff of SynHAT.
Table 6. Performance comparison with different time intervals in the first stage, where lower results are better.
Table 4. Use Case 2: Data Augmentation (50% real HATs and 50% synthesis HATs for Training)
Fig. 7. Dual-branch Drift-Jitter TempoGate Block (DJTG Block) in LST-UNet
Table 8. Transition Evaluation. The best results on each dataset are in bold, and the second-best results are underlined.

Fig. 10. Radar plots of trajectory fidelity metrics across four cities (TKY, NYC, ATX, STO). Each subplot illustrates model performance on five evaluation dimensions. To enable fair cross-metric comparison, all metric values are linearly rescaled within each city using inverted min–max normalization so that higher values correspond to better fidelity (i.e., smaller JSDs). The closer a value is to 1, the better the performance; therefore, larger polygon areas indicate superior overall performance in reproducing real-world mobility patterns.

Table 7. Efficiency versus granularity (Stage 1 variable) for TKY (28 days), NYC (14 days), ATX (7 days), and STO (56 days). Batch size = 8. Units shown in cells: G = GFLOPs, MB = megabytes. S1/S2 FLOPs are per-batch; FLOPs/HAT = (S1 + S2)/8.
Fig. 8. HAT generation fidelity performance comparison in NYC and TKY.
